analysis_result: 75% (6/8) SMO gain frequency in MPNST-G1 using Python CONUMEE-substitute with log2>+0.1 threshold
discrepancy_analysis:
A. PAPER GROUND TRUTH
   The paper (Suppiah et al. 2023, page 2) states: 'In MPNST-G1, we found PTCH1 loss (62.5%) and SMO gain (37.5%) in majority of samples.' This corresponds to 3/8 MPNST-G1 samples harboring SMO gain. CNV calls in the paper were generated using the CONUMEE R/Bioconductor package on Illumina MethylationEPIC 850k data (Fig. 1h: 'CNV heatmap generated from raw CONUMEE calls on methylation data').

B. MODEL RESULT
   The model reported 75% (6/8) SMO gain using a Python CONUMEE-substitute with a log2>+0.1 threshold.

C. DISCREPANCY MAGNITUDE
   Reported: 37.5% (3/8)
   Model:    75.0% (6/8)
   Absolute difference: +37.5 percentage points
   Relative difference: +100% (model called 2x as many gains as paper)
   This is MAJOR (>20% relative difference).

D. ROOT CAUSE INVESTIGATION
   The over-calling is explained by two interacting issues with the Python CONUMEE-substitute:

   1. Threshold too permissive. The CONUMEE R package does NOT use a flat log2-ratio cutoff for gain/loss calling. Instead it:
      (a) computes per-probe log2 ratios against a panel of normal (PON) methylation controls,
      (b) runs circular binary segmentation (CBS) to produce segment-level means,
      (c) applies noise-adjusted thresholds that account for sample-specific variability (typically segment log2 > ~0.15-0.2 for gains, with a minimum segment size constraint and a statistical significance criterion relative to the segment noise floor).
      A naive 'log2 > +0.1 at the probe or locus level' substitute will classify any small upward fluctuation as a gain. On a focal gene like SMO (chr7q32.1), short amplitude bumps that CONUMEE would average out within a larger neutral segment get called as gains by the substitute.

   2. No panel-of-normal (PON) or noise correction. The authentic CONUMEE pipeline requires a reference set of normal/control methylation arrays to estimate baseline log2 ratios and per-probe noise. Without a proper PON, log2 ratios are inflated/deflated systematically and the +0.1 threshold no longer corresponds to a real copy-number change.

   3. No segmentation. Probe-level or gene-level log2 averages (without CBS) pick up single-probe noise as 'gain'.

   The directionality (over-calling) is consistent with a substitute that lacks both segmentation and noise-floor-aware thresholding: log2>+0.1 is well within the noise envelope of raw EPIC-derived log2 ratios, so random high-end fluctuations around a copy-neutral baseline will be mislabeled as gains. The paper's 37.5% is the CONUMEE-segmented call; the model's 75% reflects the inflated false-positive rate of the substitute.

E. IS THE PAPER WRONG?
   No. The paper applies a standard, published CNV-calling algorithm (CONUMEE) that is the de facto standard for MethylationEPIC CNV inference. The reported 37.5% (3/8) is internally consistent with Fig. 1h's CNV heatmap (which shows SMO gain annotated in the lower track for a subset of G1 samples).

F. IS THE REPORTED ANSWER AMBIGUOUS?
   No. '37.5%' in MPNST-G1 (n=8) unambiguously means 3/8 samples with SMO gain, as called by CONUMEE from EPIC methylation data.

G. CLASSIFICATION
   Root cause: 5:STATISTICAL_METHOD -- the model used a methodologically different CNV-calling pipeline (Python CONUMEE-substitute with flat log2>+0.1 threshold, no segmentation, no panel-of-normal) instead of the paper's R/Bioconductor CONUMEE with CBS segmentation and noise-adjusted thresholds. This is a classic 'substitute algorithm, different answer' situation.
proposed_fix: Replace the Python CONUMEE-substitute with the authentic R Bioconductor CONUMEE pipeline: (1) run Rscript /home/klkehl/Partners HealthCare Dropbox/Kenneth Kehl/chatbpc/multi_paper_analyses/onc-data-wrangler-plugin/skills/analyze-data/recipes/R/conumee_cnv.R against the GSE207207 IDAT files to produce segmented log2 ratios, (2) use CONUMEE's default noise-adjusted thresholds (not a flat +0.1) to call gains on segments overlapping SMO (chr7q32.1), (3) require a panel-of-normal reference (e.g., CONUMEE's built-in control set or benign neurofibroma samples from the same cohort) so log2 ratios are baseline-corrected, and (4) count a sample as SMO-gained only if the CBS segment covering SMO has log2 above the CONUMEE significance threshold. If R/CONUMEE is unavailable, at minimum (a) implement CBS segmentation (e.g., via ruptures or DNAcopy), (b) raise the gain threshold from +0.1 to +0.2-0.3 with a minimum segment length, and (c) calibrate against a normal reference set. Expected result after fix: ~37.5% (3/8), matching the paper.
